input_text: Insulin is required for all patients with type 1 diabetes mellitus because they do not produce adequate insulin (due to destruction of the pancreatic beta-cells) and will develop ketoacidosis without it.

_entries.subject_type_definition: A clinical sign or physiological state in which the body produces high levels of ketone bodies due to a lack of insulin.

input_text: Insulin is also used in the management of many patients with type 2 diabetes.

input_text: Insulin replacement in type 1 diabetes should ideally mimic beta-cell function to provide basal and prandial requirements (physiologic replacement or basal-bolus dosing).

input_text: To achieve this, different preparations of insulin and methods of administration can be used.
Except for use of regular insulin, which is given IV in hospitalized patients, insulin is almost always administered subcutaneously.
An inhaled insulin preparation is available for patients who prefer not to inject themselves.
It has a slightly more rapid onset of action compared to subcutaneously injected rapid acting insulin.
Multiple daily subcutaneous injections administered by the patient, with 2 preparations of insulin used depending on the anticipated glycemic control needs.
An insulin pump that delivers a rapid- or short-acting insulin and administers a basal rate of insulin and additional boluses with meals or for correcting a high blood glucose level.

input_text: Both strategies require close attention to diet and exercise as well as to insulin timing and dose.
